channel.title: My Reading List on rahuldevajji
channel.description: Recent content in My Reading List on rahuldevajji

item.title: The Diary of Frida Kahlo: An Intimate Self-Portrait
item.description: The introductions are dense, but once you get through them you can peer into Frida Kahlo’s mind. Seeing her artistic process and raw sketches is wonderful!. Some of her drafts would probably be works of art even today.

item.title: Garden Spells
item.description: This is such a nice, heartwarming book! It was a short read, and I love a story with a happy ending. While I usually find it hard to read books that deal with trauma, I appreciated how gently this book handled those heavier themes without making it overwhelming. I would love to read more of such comforting and hopeful books.

item.title: Semiosis
item.description: The concept of first contact, but with plants as the dominant life form instead of animals, is quite interesting. It instantly reminded me of Tchaikovsky’s “Children of Time,” which I absolutely loved. Reading about plants communicating with each other and sharing resources is interesting. But I would have loved for it to have dived deeper into the science of how these alien plants work. I think there is a line between being science-heavy, like the “Three Body Problem” trilogy (making it read like a memorandum), and being a fantasy book where all the “science” is just make-believe, and I wish this book had explored more of the plant physiology.

item.title: A Cloud a Day
item.description: This book is delightful! It made me look up at the sky more. It’s a perfect road‑trip companion, because it will keep you occupied trying to identify everything you see in the sky.

item.title: Land of Mirrors
item.description: I read this on a flight back from the Middle East. I felt like a child, fascinated by the book’s wonderful artwork. At times, I sensed a deeper message that I was probably missing. I appreciated the depictions of loneliness, but I suppose I missed some of the deeper philosophical themes. When I got back, I did a quick Google search and found that others felt the same, or suggested the author intended the book to be open to multiple interpretations.

item.title: This Is How You Lose the Time War
item.description: I haven’t picked up a romance novel in a long time, but this book was the perfect one to rekindle your joy for romance. It takes the classic “enemies-to-lovers” trope and turns it into something entirely unique, with a slice of time travel. The story follows two rival agents, Red and Blue, who belong to warring futures. They travel through time to unmake each other’s work, but along the way, they begin leaving letters.

item.title: A Psalm For The Wild Built
item.description: I loved the description of the world in this series. It’s a utopian story of a world which has genuinely nice people, and everybody’s living in perfect harmony, all sunshine, and rainbows. I read this book during a gloomy fall week, at a time when I was feeling pretty low about my work and life in general. The story follows Sibling Dex, a monk whose life revolves around traveling, brewing tea, and listening to people’s problems.

item.title: Free Will
item.description: Okay, I read this book in almost 6 months ago (at the time of writing this review on 03/2026). Looking back on it, I feel I learnt nothing. The author argues put together some shower thoughts, cited some old research, and called it done. I guess it is pretty short because he doesn’t have enough things to say about this topic, it is even more evident based on how he decides to end the book “I’m hungry, yes, but it also seems that I’ve made my point.

item.title: Orbital
item.description: Orbital: A View from Above I finished the book, with some difficulty. This book reminded me of an event from my childhood. I might have been 5 years old when my mom tasked me with splitting almonds using a huge tailor’s scissors. Naturally I sliced up a piece of my left index finger (whose mark I still carry today). After crying, and getting it bandaged, my father sat me in front of his laptop with Google Earth software showing Mars.

item.title: The Whale and the Reactor
item.description: Reading Langdon Winner’s The Whale and the Reactor today feels like looking in a mirror. Published in 1986, his thoughts on technology’s place in society resonate with an unsettling familiarity. The anxieties and arguments surrounding computers and nuclear power then are pretty similar to our conversations about Artificial Intelligence now. It seems we’re grappling with the same fundamental questions, just with a new set of tools. The Inevitable Steamroller Winner criticized the idea of technology as an unstoppable force, a kind of progress we have no choice but to accept.

item.title: Crossings
item.description: The book details the multifaceted harm caused by roads, extending far beyond the estimated one million animals killed by vehicles daily in the U.S. alone. Roads fragment habitats, preventing creatures from migrating for food and mates; they serve as conduits for invasive species; and their associated salt runoff and traffic noise pollute ecosystems. Goldfarb showcases projects like wildlife bridges in California and tunnels for toads in England, demonstrating how thoughtful design can mitigate destructive effects.
